skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X tasks through the inference.sh belt CLI, including posts, media posts, likes, retweets, direct messages, follows, profile lookups, and post deletion.
skill.summary: Run Twitter/X posting and engagement workflows through authenticated belt CLI apps.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly found external command and network indicators, but many Ruby backtick and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from markdown formatting and descriptive text. The real risk is that the skill authorizes Bash(belt *) and instructs authenticated Twitter/X actions such as posting, liking, retweeting, sending DMs, following users, and deleting posts.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated External CLI Can Modify Twitter/X Accounts
medium_findings.description: The skill permits Bash(belt *) and provides belt commands that post tweets, create media posts, like posts, retweet, send DMs, follow users, and delete posts. This is legitimate automation, but misuse could publish unwanted content, spam users, or change account state without careful human review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and example commands directly show authenticated account-modifying actions. The behavior appears intentional for a social automation skill, so this is a medium operational risk rather than confirmed malicious activity.

medium_findings.title: Generated Media Workflow Invokes Additional External Apps
medium_findings.description: The image and video workflows call separate belt apps before posting their outputs to Twitter/X. This expands the external service surface and could publish generated media if the user does not verify the result first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly invoke external generation apps and then reuse resulting media URLs in posting commands. There is no evidence of data theft, but the workflow increases third-party execution and publication risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs Are Service and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to inference.sh documentation, an installation guide, an example media URL placeholder, and a hosted image. They confirm network dependency but do not show credential exfiltration or malicious endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL context is visible in markdown links and a placeholder media field. I found no evidence that the URLs receive secrets or hidden payloads.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators at the description and table header lines. Those lines contain no cryptographic code, hashing call, encryption library, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain markdown frontmatter and a table header. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are markdown code fences or inline command examples, not executable Ruby code inside the skill file. The underlying belt commands still require careful use because an agent may run them through the allowed Bash tool.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is clearly markdown fenced code, not Ruby source. The finding is partly useful as a command-execution indicator, but the Ruby-specific interpretation is false.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Bash Allowance for belt Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash(belt *), allowing any belt subcommand rather than only the listed Twitter/X app commands. This can run additional belt operations outside the documented examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration is explicit and broad within the belt command namespace. The impact depends on the installed belt CLI and user authentication state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Account State Changes Through External Network Services
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented commands depend on inference.sh and Twitter/X integrations to change account state over the network. Users should require confirmation before publishing, messaging, following, retweeting, liking, or deleting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation lists specific app IDs for publishing, engagement, DMs, following, lookup, and deletion. This is expected functionality but has direct account-impacting consequences.

content.value_statement: Managing Twitter/X activity across posts, media, replies, and account actions can be repetitive. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through belt CLI workflows for controlled social automation.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to authenticate with the belt CLI before using Twitter/X apps.
Provides commands for posting plain text tweets and media posts.
Documents engagement actions for liking, retweeting, following users, and sending direct messages.
Includes lookup commands for user profiles and individual posts.
Demonstrates workflows that generate image or video content before posting it.
limitations: Requires the belt CLI and a configured inference.sh account.
Requires valid Twitter/X integration permissions for account-changing actions.
Does not include built-in approval gates, rate limits, or moderation checks.
Does not verify generated media quality before publishing.

use_cases.title: Publish Campaign Updates
use_cases.description: Create and post approved text or media updates from a repeatable CLI workflow.

use_cases.title: Manage Community Engagement
use_cases.description: Like, retweet, follow, and message selected accounts after manual review.

use_cases.title: Prototype Social Media Agents
use_cases.description: Test account automation flows that combine generated media with Twitter/X posting.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Draft Tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a belt command to post this approved tweet text: [tweet text]. Show the command and ask before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Media Post
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a Twitter/X media post with this text and media URL: [text], [media URL]. Confirm the final post before execution.

prompt_templates.title: Review Engagement Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a reviewed engagement plan for these tweet IDs and usernames: [items]. Separate likes, retweets, follows, and messages.

prompt_templates.title: Generate and Publish Media
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow that generates media from this prompt, reviews the result, and then prepares a Twitter/X post: [creative brief].

output_examples.input: Post an approved launch update to Twitter/X.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a belt posting command, repeats the final tweet text, and waits for confirmation before running it.

output_examples.input: Like two posts and follow one account after review.
output: The assistant lists each requested account action in plain language.
It flags that the actions affect the live account.
It asks for approval before using the belt CLI.

output_examples.input: Generate an image and post it with a short caption.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a two-step workflow: generate the media first, review the media URL, then prepare the Twitter/X post.

best_practices: Require explicit approval before any post, message, follow, like, retweet, or delete action.
Review generated text and media for accuracy, rights, and brand safety before publishing.
Use test accounts or drafts when validating new automation workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not run bulk engagement commands without rate-limit and policy review.
Do not send direct messages from generated text without human approval.
Do not post generated media until the final asset has been inspected.

faq.question: Does this skill post directly to Twitter/X?
faq.answer: It provides belt CLI commands that can post when the user has authenticated access and approves execution.

faq.question: Does it require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill depends on inference.sh belt apps and the configured Twitter/X integration.

faq.question: Can it send direct messages?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented x/dm-send app can send direct messages when account permissions allow it.

faq.question: Can it delete posts?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented x/post-delete app can delete a post by ID, so confirmation is important.

faq.question: Is it safe for unattended automation?
faq.answer: No. Use human approval for live account actions, especially publishing, messaging, follows, and deletion.

faq.question: Does it include scheduling logic?
faq.answer: The description mentions scheduling use cases, but the file mainly documents direct belt app commands.
